text: The video shows the diseased ileocolic area with visible colonic and small bowel margins. An inflammatory and fistulizing mass is observed fistulizing to the duodenum.

The surgeon identifies the base of the iliopsoas artery and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) in the surgical field. The duodenum is visible, and the surgeon performs distal and proximal transections around the affected area.
The surgeon removes tissue from the duodenum, minimizing the size of the duodenal opening beyond the fistula.
The surgeon prepares to close the surgical site using a 2-0 suture, performing an anastomosis in the colon. The duodenum is positioned as a patch to cover the repair area.

The surgical field reveals severe fistulizing disease with visible fistulas connecting the proximal bowel and colon to the surrounding area. The surgeon prepares to create a diverging ileostomy to manage the condition.

The surgeon places a purse-string suture and creates a side-to-end ileocolic anastomosis using a 31mm circular stapler. Following the resection of the sigmoid colon, a colorectal anastomosis is also performed with the same 31mm circular stapler.

The video shows an ileocolic resection with multiple anteroenteric fistulas. The transverse colon, duodenum, and proximal small bowel are visibly fistulizing to the resection area. Multiple holes are observed in the ileosigmoid region, indicating fistulization.
